s: First air-conditioned Texas theatre; stars placed by National Geographic
w: John Eberson's 1929 atmospheric palace gave Texas its first air-conditioned house. The ceiling fakes a midnight sky - stars positioned with National Geographic's help, a cloud projector drifting above a painted Spanish village. Opening night Jimmie Rodgers took 18 curtain calls. Second-largest US movie house on day one.
_facts: Opened June 14, 1929; designed by John Eberson for Interstate Theatres
First fully air-conditioned theatre in Texas
Atmospheric design: fake sky ceiling with stars positioned by National Geographic consultants and cloud projector
Opening act Jimmie Rodgers received 18 curtain calls
Second-largest movie theater in the US at opening (only Atlanta's Fox was larger)
NRHP listed October 1, 1975

s: Mesoamerican revival palace with Montezuma meeting Cortés on the fire screen
w: Opened June 1926, drawing from Aztec, Mixtec, Zapotec, Toltec and Mayan sources: vibrantly-coloured columns, sculptures and murals reproducing pre-Columbian artefacts. The fire screen depicts Montezuma II receiving Cortés in 1519. The three-ton lobby chandelier was installed the day the stock market crashed, 1929.
_facts: Opened June 4, 1926; designed by Meyer & Holler
Mesoamerican revival style drawing from Aztec, Mixtec, Zapotec, Toltec and Mayan cultures
Fire screen painting depicts Montezuma II meeting Hernán Cortés in 1519
Three-ton chandelier installed October 1929 - same day as the Wall Street Crash
Now a Live Nation concert venue

s: World's first skyscraper built with air conditioning as original equipment
w: January 1928: no building had ever been constructed with AC as original equipment. The 21-storey Milam changed that, its Carrier 'Manufactured Weather' system fed by basement ice, an 18-foot chiller running 60 years. Designed by a Frank Lloyd Wright student. Landlords immediately charged 15 percent premium for the cool offices.
_facts: Opened January 1928; first building in the world designed with AC from the ground up
Designed by George Willis, a student of Frank Lloyd Wright
Carrier 'Manufactured Weather' system; ice in basement fed 18-foot chiller until October 1989
Landlords charged 10-15% rent premium for air-conditioned offices
Named for Texas Revolution leader Benjamin Milam

s: Gothic grotesques on the tallest eight-sided skyscraper in the US
w: Ayres & Ayres wrapped their 1929 tower in Late Gothic Revival brick and terra-cotta, capping it with green Ludowici tile and stone grotesques. At 404 feet, the city's tallest until 1968 - and still the tallest eight-sided structure in the United States. In 1934 a jewellery counter sold LBJ his wedding ring; 12 were tried, the winner cost $2.98.
_facts: Completed 1929 by Ayres & Ayres; Late Gothic Revival, octagonal brick and terra-cotta
Stone grotesques (often misidentified as gargoyles) and green Ludowici tile roof
Tallest eight-sided structure in the United States
San Antonio's tallest building 1929-1968 at 404 feet
In 1934 LBJ's postmaster friend bought 12 wedding rings here; the $2.98 ring fit Lady Bird

s: One of the oldest US cathedrals; Santa Anna flew no-quarter from its tower
w: Cornerstone 1738; Mexican stoneworkers arrived in 1748 after the Viceroy donated 12,000 pesos. Artillery positioned here during the 1835 Battle of Bexar, then Santa Anna raised his blood-red no-quarter flag on February 23, 1836. The dome is the geographic centre of the city. A 24-minute light projection still plays on the facade every night.
_facts: Cornerstone laid May 11, 1738; dedicated 1755
Professional stoneworkers arrived from Mexico 1748 after 12,000-peso Viceroy donation
Artillery positioned here during 1835 Battle of Bexar
Santa Anna raised no-quarter flag from the tower February 23, 1836
Dome is geographic centre of San Antonio
Video art projection 'San Antonio: The Saga' on facade since 2014

s: Solar-aligned door floods the interior on the Feast of the Assumption yearly
w: Founded in East Texas in 1711, relocated here in 1731. The western entrance aligns so precisely that a double solar illumination fills the interior on August 15 - the Feast of the Assumption - each year. Original painted frescoes survive on the walls and ceilings. The first major battle of the Texas Revolution was fought here on October 28, 1835.
_facts: Founded 1711 in East Texas; relocated to San Antonio 1731
Western entrance creates annual double solar illumination on August 15 (Feast of the Assumption)
Original painted frescoes survive on interior ceilings and walls
Battle of Concepcion October 28, 1835: first major engagement of the Texas Revolution
Part of UNESCO World Heritage San Antonio Missions (2015)

s: Franciscan stone aqueduct built in 1731, still carrying water today
w: Franciscan friars completed this gravity-flow arch in 1731, adapting Roman and Moorish irrigation technology for the Texas brush country. Part of a 15-mile acequia network that watered 3,500 acres, it still delivers water to the mission's former farmlands. A National Historic Civil Engineering Landmark that simply never stopped working.
_facts: Built 1731 by Franciscan friars for Mission Espada
Part of 15-mile acequia network irrigating 3,500 acres
Gravity-flow technology derived from Roman/Moorish irrigation methods brought to Spain
Still functioning nearly 300 years later
National Historic Civil Engineering Landmark and National Historic Landmark (July 19, 1964)

s: Texas's oldest public park, on the spring where San Antonio was founded
w: The Payaya called this spring-fed site Yanaguana long before Europeans arrived. Spanish missionaries settled here in 1718 to found San Antonio. The 46-acre park that grew around the springs is the oldest in Texas, a state landmark since 1965 and on the National Register since 1979. The water still rises from the same ground.
_facts: Oldest park in Texas at 46 acres around natural springs
Originally Payaya Indian settlement called Yanaguana
Site of the 1718 founding of San Antonio
Recorded Texas Historic Landmark since 1965
Added to National Register of Historic Places November 1, 1979

s: WPA outdoor stage where the river itself divides performers from their crowd
w: Robert Hugman's 1941 WPA design splits stage from audience across the actual river: performers on the north bank, 800 spectators on grass terraces on the south, a stone bridge woven into productions. Named for a WPA engineer who died before ground was broken. The 'Father of the River Walk' rang the bells bearing his name two years before his death.
_facts: Built 1939-1941 by the WPA; stage on north bank, grass-terraced audience on south bank
Named after Edwin P. Arneson, WPA engineer who died before construction began
Supervised by Robert Hugman, architect of the River Walk
Hugman rang bells named for him for the first time two years before his death
Featured in 2000 film Miss Congeniality

s: Texas's first modern art museum, bequeathed inside a mansion on 23 acres
w: Marion Koogler McNay left her 24-room Spanish Colonial Revival mansion and grounds to the public at her death; in 1954 it opened as the first modern art museum in Texas. Fountains, broad lawns, a Japanese-inspired fishpond and a collection from Cézanne to O'Keeffe - all in an estate that remains surreally domestic.
_facts: First modern art museum in Texas, opened 1954
Established by bequest of Marion Koogler McNay
24-room Spanish Colonial Revival mansion on 23 acres
Features fountains, Japanese-inspired garden and fishpond
Collection spans Cézanne, Picasso, O'Keeffe and other modern artists

s: World-class Latin American collection inside an 1886 River Walk brewery
w: The Lone Star Brewery of 1886 stood empty until a $7.2 million conversion opened it as SAMA in March 1981. Inside: 30,000 objects across five millennia, anchored by the Nelson Rockefeller Latin American trove - 7,000 folk art objects among the world's most significant holdings. The 1913 streetcar that once shuttled visitors was sold to an Oregon trolley club.
_facts: Occupies former Lone Star Brewery building, 1886
Opened March 1981 after $7.2 million renovation
Over 30,000 objects spanning 5,000 years
Nelson Rockefeller Latin American collection (acquired 1985): 7,000+ folk art objects
1913 streetcar 'Old 300' ran heritage service 1982-1985; sold to Oregon

s: Underground greenhouse sunk into one of the city's highest points
w: The Lucile Halsell Conservatory, opened 1988, is the centrepiece: a subterranean greenhouse by Argentine architect Emilio Ambasz with five climate-specific pavilions below grade, its glass cones barely visible from above. The 41-acre garden occupies a former limestone quarry 798 feet up. A Victorian carriage house was moved here brick by brick from downtown.
_facts: Opened May 3, 1980; 41 acres on former limestone quarry (c.798 ft elevation)
Lucile Halsell Conservatory opened February 29, 1988; designed by Emilio Ambasz
Subterranean design: five climate-specific pavilions below grade
Won several architectural design awards
Sullivan Carriage House relocated brick by brick from downtown in 1988

s: WPA limestone amphitheatre in a quarry — the 'Rock Pile' since 1940
w: Built by the WPA in an abandoned quarry, this horseshoe of limestone has been the 'Rock Pile' since opening night drew 24,000 for a high school football doubleheader in 1940. Record crowd was 26,208 in 1949. Four defunct professional leagues have played here. The National Register made it official in 2011.
_facts: Opened September 20, 1940; WPA-funded limestone construction on abandoned rock quarry
Nicknamed 'the Rock Pile'
Opening night drew 24,000; record attendance 26,208 on November 11, 1949
Third-largest high school stadium in Texas (18,500 after 2011 renovation)
NRHP listed September 8, 2011; has hosted WFL, USFL, NASL and other professional teams

s: Home of one of only two native-born Texans to sign the Declaration
w: Jose Antonio Navarro built his limestone and adobe compound here in the barrio of Laredito between 1832 and 1855. He signed the Texas Declaration of Independence in 1836 - one of only two native-born Texans to do so - then in 1841 was captured, sentenced to death and imprisoned in Mexico, before escaping with the help of sympathetic officers.
_facts: Jose Antonio Navarro (1795-1871) built compound c.1832-1855 in former Tejano barrio Laredito
One of only two native-born Texans to sign the Texas Declaration of Independence (1836)
Captured after 1841 Texan Santa Fe Expedition; sentenced to death, imprisoned in Mexico
Escaped with help of sympathetic Mexican Army officials
National Historic Landmark since 2016; NRHP listed 1972

s: Where Teddy Roosevelt drank and recruited his Rough Riders in 1898
w: William Menger, a German brewer, opened this hotel next to the Alamo in 1859. The bar became legend when Roosevelt drank there and recruited his Rough Riders in 1898. A Confederate supply post in the Civil War, nearly demolished for a car park during WWII, still serving Mary Menger's mango ice cream. She once sent wagons round town to collect businessmen for dinner.
_facts: Opened February 1859 by William and Mary Menger
Teddy Roosevelt recruited Rough Riders at the Menger Bar in 1898
Confederate supply station during the Civil War
Nearly demolished for a parking lot during WWII
Mary Menger sent wagons to collect businessmen for dinner
Original mango ice cream still served today

s: Second-oldest US airport in continuous operation, opened by women in 1915
w: The Stinson sisters opened their flying school here in October 1915 after the Army commandeered Fort Sam Houston's airfield to watch the Mexican border. Katherine financed it; Marjorie taught the first five students - all Canadians. Civilian flights banned entirely during WWI. Renamed Windburn Field in 1927, it reverted to Stinson Field in 1936.
_facts: Established October 1915; second-oldest general aviation airport in continuous US operation
Katherine Stinson financed it; sister Marjorie Stinson was the flight instructor
Relocated from Fort Sam Houston after the 1st Aero Squadron took the fort for border surveillance
First class of five Canadians graduated November 1915
Civilian flights banned during WWI; renamed Windburn Field 1927, reverted to Stinson 1936

s: The Queen of the Missions, its Rose Window carved for a love lost at sea
w: Father Antonio Margil founded San José in 1720; the church completed in 1768 is the grandest of the four surviving missions. Its sacristy Rose Window is Spanish Baroque carving at its finest. Legend holds that sculptor Pedro Huizar carved it as a memorial to Rosa, the sweetheart who died at sea before she could join him. UNESCO World Heritage since 2015.
_facts: Founded 1720 by Father Antonio Margil; church completed 1768
Called 'Queen of the Missions'
Rose Window on the sacristy: outstanding Spanish Baroque carved stonework
Legend: sculptor Pedro Huizar carved it in memory of Rosa, who died at sea
UNESCO World Heritage Site (July 5, 2015)

s: Home of the world's largest Tejano conjunto festival and its Hall of Fame
w: Founded in 1979, GCAC moved into the Teatro Guadalupe, a 1940 theatre shuttered for 14 years, revived for the Chicano arts movement. Since 1982 it has run the Tejano Conjunto Festival - the world's largest, drawing 50,000 people at its peak - and maintains the Conjunto Music Hall of Fame. The largest community-based multidisciplinary arts organisation in the US.
_facts: Founded 1979; first home was Teatro Guadalupe, a 1940 theatre closed for 14 years
One of the first Chicano organisations to collaborate with Mexican artists
Tejano Conjunto Festival established 1982; drew 50,000 over six days at peak
Maintains Conjunto Music Hall of Fame
Described as largest community-based multidisciplinary arts organisation in the US
